title: ULPower Valve Cover
subtitle: Reverse-engineered aircraft engine valve cover — Dassault Systèmes
summary: Reverse-engineered a missing ULPower aircraft engine valve cover from calipers and reference photos into a production-ready CAD model.
why_md: While working on-site at Dassault Systèmes, I was tasked with recreating a missing ULPower aircraft engine valve cover. This wasn't a "find a replacement" job — buying one wasn't an option. It had to be reverse-engineered from scratch, using only measurements, reference images, and my ability to translate them into an accurate, manufacturable CAD model. This was more than just copying a part. It was about producing a file that could go straight into the prototyping and production pipeline without requiring additional modifications.
how_md: Capturing the geometry required both precision and creativity. I used calipers to take every measurable dimension I could, then referenced photos to model the subtle curves, embossed lettering, and gasket sealing surfaces in CAD. Because this was for an aircraft engine, I had to account for real-world constraints like vibration, thermal expansion, and material removal during machining. I printed a prototype in PLA+ on my Ender 3 V2 Neo to verify the fit and alignment before handing off the final file for production.
problems_md: The completed model matched the original in form, function, and tolerance requirements, making it ready for manufacturing. This project reinforced my ability to bridge physical measurements and digital modeling, while thinking ahead to how the part will perform in an operational environment. It also showed I can work under constraints and deliver a solution that meets both engineering and production standards.

title: Monitor Mount
subtitle: FEA-driven mount for a 7.1 kg, 31.5″ display
summary: An FEA-driven monitor mount: 8 iterations to a factor of safety of 2–5 and under 0.3 mm displacement carrying a 7.1 kg display.
why_md: I designed this monitor mount to support a 7.1 kg, 31.5-inch Acer monitor while keeping the ports easy to access. The concept started with a simple block that locked the monitor at 90 degrees, but I wanted the ability to swivel it up for convenience. From the start I knew PETG would be strong enough, so the challenge became finding the right balance between strength and efficiency by cutting weight where possible while maintaining a high factor of safety.
how_md: My first simulation showed that a solid PETG block would easily handle the weight, so I began reducing material in controlled ways. Triangular supports set just three millimeters from the walls carried the load well, and additional cutouts only improved force distribution. To push the design, I ran a study with 100 newtons pushing from the top and another 100 newtons pulling through the hinge pin. The results came back mostly in blue and light green, showing plenty of safety margin, with only a small stress spot on one triangle. Instead of adding more infill, I simplified the geometry and added a triangle cutout behind the hinge hole, which increased strength without extra material. I was also concerned about twisting forces being too much for a single screw, so I added a second one and moved them apart to distribute the load. After several iterations I rethought the hinge placement and found a better position that gave me a cleaner load path.
problems_md: After eight design iterations and many simulations the mount consistently showed a factor of safety between two and five. Maximum displacement was measured at only 0.3 millimeters for the wall piece and 0.02 millimeters for the screen mount, well within safe limits. The process reinforced my approach of **simplify then lighten**: remove unnecessary material, reinforce only where the data shows weakness, and trust the results. The final mount is efficient, reliable, and able to hold my monitor in place with confidence.

title: Raspberry Pi Laptop
subtitle: A SolidWorks-designed Linux laptop on a Raspberry Pi
summary: A fully functional Linux laptop on a Raspberry Pi — five hinge iterations until the screen sat exactly right.
why_md: This project began as part of a SolidWorks class, but I treated it like a real-world product from day one. I set out to design and build a fully functional Linux laptop powered by a Raspberry Pi, aiming for something practical, affordable, and portable that didn't feel like a rough prototype. I wanted an enclosure that looked clean, routed cables internally without tangles, and gave full access to every necessary port. This wasn't just about putting parts in a box — it was a complete system designed to work reliably while looking professional.
how_md: Designing a laptop hinge became one of the toughest problems to solve. It took five different iterations before I found one that could hold the screen at multiple angles without wobble, while still folding smoothly when closed. Inside, I had to position the Pi, battery, and display in a way that prevented cable strain and kept all ports perfectly aligned with the enclosure openings. I took part in the majority of the CAD modeling and R&D, from the main body and lid geometry to optimize airflow to the mounting features and internal cable channels.
problems_md: The final laptop was compact, boot-ready, and felt solid in hand. It proved I could own a project from concept to execution while balancing usability, manufacturability, and aesthetics. Beyond the hardware, it showed that I can combine CAD design, mechanical problem solving, and hands-on assembly into one cohesive build that works as well in practice as it does on paper.

Final render of the Raspberry Pi laptop

title: Carbon Filter Fume Extractor
subtitle: A $5 carbon-filter fume extractor that beat the commercial unit
summary: A compact carbon-filter fume extractor built for about $5 that outperformed commercial units at roughly 10× the price.
why_md: I built this compact carbon-filter fume extractor to solve a real problem in my home workshop — poor ventilation when soldering. Commercial options at the low end were underpowered and overpriced, so I decided to design and build one myself using materials I already had. The goal was a unit that was small, efficient, and reliable, with thoughtful design choices that made it easy to maintain.
how_md: The first design included a stand for adjustable angles, but testing proved the fan's airflow was strong enough to pull fumes from four inches away without repositioning. Removing the stand simplified the build and improved print orientation. Initially, I used a 12 V fan and a voltage booster, but repeated failures during testing (confirmed with multimeter readings) showed the setup was overstressing components. I switched to a 5 V USB-C fan that reduced electrical load, simplified wiring, and eliminated the problematic booster circuit entirely.
problems_md: The finished extractor outperformed similar commercial units, cost me about five dollars to build, and could be serviced easily if needed. More importantly, it taught me how small design changes can have big performance impacts, and how to merge airflow efficiency, electronics troubleshooting, and structural design into a single, well-balanced product.

gallery.alt: Carbon filter fume extractor beauty shot

title: OctoPi Laptop Enclosure
subtitle: A dedicated OctoPi control station for 3D-printer management
summary: A portable, single-purpose OctoPi enclosure — Pi, display, camera, and integrated cooling in one compact unit.
why_md: I wanted a portable, dedicated station for running OctoPi so I could manage my 3D printer without tying up my main computer. Inspired by my Raspberry Pi laptop build, I reworked the concept for a single-purpose device optimized for printing control. The design needed to be compact, durable, and organized, keeping everything in one unit that could be moved around the workspace easily.
how_md: I designed the enclosure to hold the Pi, a display, a camera module, and integrated cooling vents, all while keeping the footprint as small as possible. Cable routing was built directly into the design so everything stayed clean and tangle-free, making it easier to set up and service. The hinge mounts were reinforced so the lid could be opened and closed repeatedly without wearing out. Every mounting point, vent cutout, and cable channel was placed with a focus on reliability and ease of use.
problems_md: The completed enclosure became a mobile printer control hub that streamlined my workflow and kept my setup organized. It showed I can adapt an existing design into a specialized, single-purpose tool that works better for its intended task while keeping build quality high and usability front and center.

title: Filament Recycler
subtitle: Closing the loop on 3D-printing waste
summary: A beginner-friendly filament recycler that turns printing waste into usable filament — from PLA to abrasive carbon blends.
why_md: 3D printing is great until you see the pile of wasted plastic — failed prints, support material, leftover spools that can't be reused without the right gear. I want to flip that problem. This concept is a beginner-friendly, low-maintenance filament recycler that works for any material from basic PLA to abrasive carbon blends.
how_md: I would design a rugged metal hopper that can handle heavy use, feed it into a hardened shredder, and push the material through a precisely heated extrusion system. The key is making it simple enough for makers to operate but robust enough to handle different plastics without constant maintenance or expensive replacements.
problems_md: This would turn 3D-printing waste from a disposal problem into a resource loop, making additive manufacturing more sustainable and cost-effective for both hobbyists and production environments.

title: Automatic Hydroponic System
subtitle: Arduino-based precision agriculture for small-scale growers
summary: An Arduino automation unit handling EC sensing, pH tracking, and nutrient dosing for hydroponics.
why_md: Hydroponics lives and dies on precision. If your nutrient levels are off your plants either stall or burn. Manually checking EC and dosing nutrients gets old fast, especially when you have multiple systems running. My concept is an Arduino-based automation unit that handles it all — EC sensing, pH tracking, and nutrient dosing through a set of small pumps.
how_md: The probes would be modular for easy swap-outs, and all electronics would sit in a sealed enclosure to survive high humidity. I want it to learn your system's patterns over time, adjusting dosing schedules based on plant growth stages and environmental changes. The goal is set-it-and-forget-it automation that actually improves crop yields.
problems_md: This would make precision agriculture accessible to small-scale growers, reducing crop loss from nutrient imbalances while freeing up time for other critical farming tasks.

title: AI-Powered Motorcycle Helmet
subtitle: Active prevention, not just passive protection
summary: An AI-powered motorcycle helmet with hazard detection, predictive alerts, and a HUD in the rider's view.
why_md: On a bike every second matters, sometimes less than a second. This helmet concept is about making those seconds count by turning passive protection into active prevention. It would integrate AI-powered hazard detection, predictive alerts, and a heads-up display directly into the rider's view.
how_md: Using a network of small cameras and sensors it could track blind spots, monitor following distance, and highlight road hazards before they become a problem. The AI would learn your riding patterns and local road conditions, getting better at predicting dangerous situations the more you ride. All critical alerts would appear as subtle visual overlays that don't distract from the road.
problems_md: This technology could significantly reduce motorcycle accidents by giving riders the situational awareness they need to avoid dangerous situations before they escalate.

title: Automated Solar Farm Drones
subtitle: A swarm of AI-driven drones deploying solar where equipment can't reach
summary: A coordinated swarm of worker drones that transport, position, and install solar panels with minimal human help.
why_md: Building and maintaining solar farms, especially in remote areas, takes a lot of time and money. My concept is a coordinated swarm of AI-driven worker drones that can transport, position, and install solar panels with very little human help.
how_md: Each drone would have a role — some for ground prep, others for carrying panels, and others for precision placement. A larger queen drone would act as a mobile base station with extra power and tools for the swarm. The idea is a self-deploying system that could set up solar installations in challenging terrain where traditional construction equipment can't operate efficiently.
problems_md: This would dramatically reduce the cost and time required to deploy renewable energy infrastructure, making solar power viable in previously inaccessible locations.

title: Off-Grid Environmental Sensor
subtitle: Solar-powered, low-cost climate monitoring at community scale
summary: A low-cost, solar-powered sensor collecting CO₂, particulates, temperature, and humidity for a decentralized network.
why_md: Most environmental monitoring is either low-tech and very local, or advanced and too expensive for wide use. My concept is a low-cost, solar-powered sensor that can run on its own while collecting high-value climate data.
how_md: Each one would measure CO₂, particulate matter, temperature, humidity, and other pollutants, sending the data securely to a decentralized network. This would let communities, researchers, and policy makers see real-time environmental maps without relying on a small number of expensive monitoring stations.
problems_md: Widespread deployment would create a comprehensive environmental monitoring network, providing critical data for climate research and public health initiatives at unprecedented scale and resolution.
